title: Listening - Part 4: Taking a Career Break
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five professionals discussing their experiences of taking a year-long sabbatical from work

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d been working as a junior doctor for three years, and [21]the constant pressure and emotional intensity of the hospital had left me feeling completely drained[/21]. I needed to step away before I lost my passion for medicine altogether. I spent my year traveling through Southeast Asia. It was incredible, but [26]the most challenging part was dealing with the persistent anxiety that I was falling behind my peers professionally[/26]. Every time I saw a colleague post about a new certification or promotion on LinkedIn, I felt a pang of panic. It took a lot of mental effort to remind myself that my well-being was more important than a slightly faster career trajectory. || Speaker 2: I’d reached a comfortable position in my marketing firm, but I felt like I was just going through the motions. [22]I took a sabbatical because I wanted to see if I could actually make it as a professional photographer[/22]. It was a 'now or never' moment. I spent the year building a portfolio and traveling. [27]What I found most difficult was the lack of a structured routine that I’d relied on for a decade[/27]. Without a boss or a set schedule, I often found myself wasting days on end. I had to learn to become my own manager, which was a much harder lesson in self-discipline than I ever expected. It was a productive year, but a very humbling one. || Speaker 3: I was a corporate lawyer and quite successful, but [23]I realized I’d become completely disconnected from my family because of my eighty-hour work weeks[/23]. I took the year off to reconnect with my children and my spouse. It was the best decision I ever made. However, [28]I was surprised by how much I struggled with the loss of status that came with my job title[/28]. When people asked 'What do you do?' at parties, saying 'I’m on a break' felt like an admission of failure. It made me realize how much of my self-worth was tied up in my professional identity, which was a bit of a wake-up call for my ego. || Speaker 4: I’m a high school teacher, and [24]I took a career break because I was fascinated by ancient history and wanted to volunteer on an archaeological dig in Egypt[/24]. I wanted to bring some real-world experience back to my classroom. It was physically grueling but fascinating. [29]The biggest hurdle was the financial strain of having no income for twelve months[/29]. I’d saved up, but unexpected expenses meant I was living on a very tight budget by the end. I had to sacrifice a lot of comforts, but the knowledge and stories I gained were worth every penny. It’s made me a much better and more engaging teacher. || Speaker 5: I’d been at the same tech company since graduation, and [25]I took a sabbatical simply because I felt I’d never had the chance to explore the world on my own terms[/25]. I wanted a year of total freedom. I went to South America and learned Spanish. [30]The most unexpected benefit was the massive boost in my creative problem-solving skills once I returned to work[/30]. Because I’d had to navigate unfamiliar cultures and languages on my own, I came back much more confident and flexible in my thinking. My boss actually noticed that I was approaching technical challenges with a completely different, more innovative mindset. It was a total reset for my brain.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) why each speaker decided to take a career break.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 felt drained by the pressure and intensity of the hospital and needed to step away.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to see if he could succeed as a professional photographer.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to reconnect with his family after years of long work weeks.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to gain real-world experience on an archaeological dig to help her teaching.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted a year of freedom to explore the world on her own terms.

options.A: to recover from a physical illness
options.B: to test the viability of a new career
options.D: to complete a specific academic course
options.G: to deepen their knowledge of a subject
options.H: to satisfy a desire for independent travel

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome or a difficulty of their break.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 was anxious about falling behind her peers professionally.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 struggled with the lack of a structured routine.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 struggled with the loss of status associated with his job title.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 found the financial strain of having no income for a year to be the biggest hurdle.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 found that her creative problem-solving and innovative thinking improved significantly.

options.B: a loss of interest in their original job
options.C: the impact of limited financial resources
options.E: the struggle to find a new position
options.F: an enhancement of their cognitive flexibility
options.G: a sense of reduced social standing
options.H: the discovery of a new social circle
